title: GnRH Agonist Treatment Failure in Central Precocious Puberty
subtitle: Flare prevention, rescue dosing, over-suppression, and LH-vs-estradiol monitoring

patient.chiefComplaint: Unsuppressed LH on leuprolide depot 11.25 mg every 90 days
pmh: Central precocious puberty on GnRH-agonist suppression
medications: Leuprolide depot 11.25 mg IM every 90 days

caseDescription: A girl with central precocious puberty on leuprolide depot 11.25 mg every 90 days is found to have an unsuppressed LH. This is a treatment failure that demands prompt protocol revision before further irreversible bone-age advancement and lost adult height. The work-up and rescue cover: how GnRH agonists work and why a flare occurs, why a 3-monthly depot can fail, flare-prevention options beyond medroxyprogesterone, the rescue strategy (switch to monthly, escalate, maximum dosing), the harms of over-suppression and how to monitor for them, and how to weigh LH versus estradiol with their target ranges.
keyFindings: On leuprolide 11.25 mg every 90 days, LH is unsuppressed (escape)
Confirm with trough labs and a GnRH-stimulated LH; check whether estradiol is also pubertal
Act now — switch to monthly dosing rather than waiting another 3-month cycle
Always exclude a peripheral (gonadotropin-independent) source if estradiol stays pubertal
finalDiagnosis: GnRH-agonist escape/treatment failure on a 3-monthly depot — manage by switching to monthly dosing, escalating dose if needed, confirming suppression, and avoiding over-suppression; reconsider peripheral precocious puberty if refractory
learningObjectives: Explain the GnRH-agonist flare-then-downregulation mechanism and prevent the flare (beyond medroxyprogesterone)
Apply a rescue protocol (monthly dosing, dose escalation, maximum doses) and confirm suppression with trough and stimulated LH
Recognise over-suppression harms and use LH and estradiol targets to keep the patient in the therapeutic window

items.title: The case: unsuppressed LH on a 3-monthly depot
items.content: A girl with central precocious puberty on leuprolide depot 11.25 mg every 90 days now has an unsuppressed LH. This is a treatment failure, not a minor lab blip: while the axis is escaping, estrogen continues to advance bone age, and every month of inadequate suppression translates into irreversible skeletal maturation and lost adult height. The correct response is to confirm the escape, switch to a more intensive regimen at once, and at the same time make sure this is genuinely central precocious puberty and not a peripheral source the agonist can never control.
keyPoints: Unsuppressed LH on a depot = treatment failure (escape), not a trivial finding.
Ongoing escape advances bone age irreversibly — act promptly.
Confirm escape, intensify therapy, and exclude a peripheral source.

items.title: How GnRH agonists work and why a flare occurs
items.content: Physiological GnRH is pulsatile (every 90-120 minutes), which is what stimulates gonadotropin synthesis and release. GnRH agonists instead deliver continuous, non-pulsatile receptor stimulation. In the first 1-2 weeks this causes a transient surge of LH/FSH and a rise in estradiol — the 'flare' (vaginal bleeding, breast tenderness, mood changes). Over the next 2-4 weeks sustained receptor occupancy internalises the receptors and depletes them below a critical threshold, so the gonadotroph becomes refractory and LH/FSH fall to prepubertal levels with estradiol suppressed and clinical regression. The therapeutic effect is downregulation; the flare is the unavoidable first phase that sometimes needs covering.
keyPoints: Pulsatile GnRH stimulates; continuous (agonist) exposure ultimately downregulates.
Flare phase: weeks 1-2 — LH/FSH/estradiol surge.
Suppression follows within weeks as receptors are internalised/depleted.

items.title: Why a 3-monthly depot can fail
items.content: The 11.25 mg 3-month depot must keep serum leuprolide above the downregulation threshold for ~12 weeks. Failure can arise from inadequate trough levels (faster metabolisers drop below threshold in the final weeks), partial escape (enough receptors recycle to the surface late in the interval to allow low-level gonadotropin secretion), incomplete initial downregulation, or an injection/technique problem (drug given into fat rather than a proper depot). Critically, it can also be that the disorder is not (or not only) central: if there is an autonomous peripheral source (McCune-Albright, a functional ovarian cyst, exogenous or adrenal estrogen), a GnRH agonist will never suppress estradiol no matter how much LH it suppresses. Identify which mechanism applies before simply escalating.
keyPoints: Inadequate trough / late partial escape across the long interval.
Incomplete initial downregulation, or faulty injection technique.
Or a peripheral source the agonist cannot control — exclude it.

items.title: What the labs are telling you
items.content: Confirm and characterise the escape. A basal/trough LH above the assay's prepubertal cutoff (often > 0.3 IU/L) indicates inadequate suppression; a random LH clearly in the pubertal range is definite escape. The gold standard is a GnRH-stimulated LH: a peak above ~4-5 IU/L proves incomplete suppression. Then look at estradiol: if it is still detectable/pubertal, the unsuppressed LH is biologically active and the ovary is responding. If LH is unsuppressed but estradiol is still suppressed, you have early escape — intervene before estradiol climbs. If LH is suppressed but estradiol is pubertal, suspect a peripheral source rather than a dosing problem.
keyPoints: Trough LH > prepubertal cutoff = inadequate suppression; stimulated LH > 4-5 = definite.
Pubertal estradiol confirms the unsuppressed LH is biologically active.
Suppressed LH + pubertal estradiol → peripheral source, not under-dosing.

items.title: Flare prophylaxis with medroxyprogesterone (MPA)
items.content: Medroxyprogesterone acetate covers the flare window by directly suppressing the pituitary gonadotroph and ovarian steroidogenesis and inducing endometrial atrophy. Dose: 5-10 mg/day orally, or a single 150 mg IM depot. Timing: start about 1 week before the first GnRH-agonist dose and continue for 2-4 weeks after it (i.e. cover the whole flare window). Route: oral daily or IM depot. It is the traditional flare-prevention agent, but it is not the only — or the most definitive — option.
keyPoints: MPA 5-10 mg/day PO, or 150 mg IM depot.
Start ~1 week before the agonist; continue 2-4 weeks to cover the flare.
Acts centrally (gonadotroph) and peripherally (ovary, endometrium).

items.title: Flare prevention beyond medroxyprogesterone
items.content: Several strategies can prevent or blunt the flare. The most definitive is GnRH-antagonist pre-treatment (e.g. cetrorelix/ganirelix/degarelix) for 1-3 days before and 1-2 weeks into agonist therapy: it competitively blocks the receptor and causes immediate suppression with NO flare — though cost and pediatric availability limit it. An aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole/letrozole) started with the agonist does not stop the LH surge but neutralises its effect by blocking estradiol synthesis, preventing the estrogen-mediated bleed. Tamoxifen (a SERM) blocks estrogen at target tissues (helps breast tenderness/mood, not bleeding). A 'step-up' approach — daily subcutaneous leuprolide 20 mcg/kg/day for 2-4 weeks then switch to depot — achieves faster, more uniform downregulation and a shorter flare; alternatively start with a monthly (not 3-monthly) depot for higher peak levels. Cyproterone acetate (where available) gives dual progestin/anti-androgen cover, and NSAIDs help cramping symptomatically.
keyPoints: Most definitive: GnRH-antagonist pre-treatment — eliminates the flare (cost/availability limited).
Aromatase inhibitor blocks the flare's estrogen effect; tamoxifen blocks estrogen action (not bleeding).
Daily 'step-up' leuprolide then depot, or a monthly (not 3-monthly) start, shortens the flare.

items.title: How to rescue unsuppressed LH on a 3-monthly depot
items.content: Do not wait another 3-month cycle. Switch to monthly dosing immediately at the next scheduled injection — leuprolide 7.5 mg (or 11.25 mg) IM every 28 days, or triptorelin 3.75 mg monthly if you suspect partial resistance to leuprolide. Then check labs at 4 weeks — at the TROUGH, just before the next dose, which is the most vulnerable point: a suppressed trough LH (and estradiol) means it is suppressed across the whole interval. If the trough is still unsuppressed, escalate the dose. The principle is uniform, unbroken receptor occupancy: monthly dosing gives higher, steadier peaks and more reliable downregulation than the long 3-monthly interval.
keyPoints: Switch to monthly dosing at the NEXT injection — don't wait 3 months.
Leuprolide 7.5 or 11.25 mg q28 days (or triptorelin 3.75 mg monthly).
Recheck at 4 weeks (trough); suppressed trough = suppressed whole interval.

items.title: Dose escalation and maximum dosing
items.content: If monthly 7.5 mg fails, escalate: leuprolide 11.25 mg monthly, then 15 mg monthly (off-label, used in refractory cases), then 22.5 mg every 2 months; alternatives are triptorelin 3.75 mg monthly or a histrelin implant. Reported maxima: leuprolide up to ~15 mg monthly (some guidelines), with 22.5 mg monthly in case reports; triptorelin up to 7.5 mg monthly; histrelin implant replaced at 9-10 months if escape at end of implant life. Weight matters: a weight-based starting dose is ~0.2-0.3 mg/kg/month (depot), and higher-BMI children may need more because the lipophilic drug is sequestered in adipose tissue (some centres use up to 0.5 mg/kg/month in refractory cases). After achieving suppression, maintain monthly dosing for 6-12 months and only return to 3-monthly once suppression is robustly confirmed (including a stimulated LH).
keyPoints: Escalate: 7.5 → 11.25 → 15 mg monthly; or 22.5 mg q2 months; or triptorelin/histrelin.
Weight-based ~0.2-0.3 mg/kg/month; higher BMI may need more (adipose sequestration).
Hold monthly for 6-12 months before considering a return to 3-monthly.

items.title: Why too much GnRH agonist is also harmful
items.content: Pushing the dose too hard creates over-suppression. Estradiol is not meant to be zero in a prepubertal child: a small amount is required, with GH/IGF-1, for normal growth-plate function and bone mineralisation. Driving estradiol below ~5 pg/mL can decelerate growth below the expected prepubertal rate, reduce bone mineral density (estrogen deficiency increases resorption — a concern with prolonged therapy), and cause vasomotor symptoms, mood changes, and possibly cognitive complaints; very profound suppression can also delay recovery of the axis after stopping. The goal is the prepubertal range, not abolition — so escalation must be checked against signs of over-suppression, not pushed to the lowest possible estradiol.
keyPoints: Estradiol should stay prepubertal (~10-20 pg/mL), not be driven to zero.
Over-suppression: growth deceleration, low bone density, vasomotor/mood effects.
Profound suppression can delay axis recovery after discontinuation.

items.title: How to know it is harming: the therapeutic window
items.content: Keep the patient between under- and over-suppression. Under-suppressed: pubertal LH, pubertal estradiol, accelerated growth, advancing bone age, clinical progression. Adequately suppressed: LH < 0.3 IU/L, estradiol prepubertal (~10-20 pg/mL), growth velocity 4-6 cm/year, bone age stabilising, clinical regression/stability. Over-suppressed: undetectable LH, estradiol < 5 pg/mL, growth velocity < 3 cm/year, complete cessation of bone maturation, vasomotor symptoms. Monitor LH (every 1-3 months initially), estradiol and growth velocity (every 3 months), bone age (every 6-12 months), and add a DEXA scan if therapy exceeds ~2 years. If over-suppression is found, reduce the dose, lengthen the interval, or deliberately allow brief end-of-interval 'mini-escape' to preserve some estrogen exposure.
keyPoints: Adequate: LH < 0.3, estradiol 10-20, growth 4-6 cm/yr, bone age stabilising.
Over-suppressed: undetectable LH, estradiol < 5, growth < 3 cm/yr — back off.
Monitor LH, estradiol, growth velocity, bone age; DEXA if > 2 years of therapy.

items.title: Estradiol versus LH: which matters more?
items.content: Both matter, but they tell different parts of the story. LH (random/trough) reflects the adequacy of central suppression and is the EARLIEST indicator of escape — LH rises before estradiol — so monitoring LH gives an early warning to intervene before clinical breakthrough. Estradiol is the bioactive endpoint that confirms whether unsuppressed LH is clinically relevant (is the ovary actually making estrogen?) and integrates with bone age and growth. A GnRH-stimulated LH is the gold-standard proof of suppression. Read them together: suppressed LH + suppressed estradiol + regression = adequate; suppressed LH + pubertal estradiol = a peripheral estrogen source (the agonist dose is fine); unsuppressed LH + suppressed estradiol = early escape (act now); unsuppressed LH + pubertal estradiol = definite failure; suppressed both but still progressing = non-compliance, technique error, or GH excess (check IGF-1).
keyPoints: LH rises before estradiol — it is the early-warning marker of escape.
Estradiol confirms biological relevance; stimulated LH is the gold standard.
Suppressed LH + pubertal estradiol = peripheral source; both suppressed + progression = check compliance/IGF-1.

items.title: Target levels: the sweet spot
items.content: Aim for prepubertal estradiol (~10-20 pg/mL) with suppressed LH — enough estrogen for normal prepubertal growth velocity (4-6 cm/year) and bone mineralisation, but not enough for pubertal progression. Random/trough LH target < 0.3 IU/L (0.3-0.6 is a grey zone warranting a GnRH stimulation test; > 0.6 likely escape). GnRH-stimulated LH ideally < 2 IU/L, with < 4 acceptable and > 4-5 inadequate (change management). Estradiol should not be persistently < 5 pg/mL (over-suppression risk to growth and bone) nor > 30 pg/mL (pubertal — intervene). Treat the combination, not a single number.
keyPoints: Sweet spot: estradiol ~10-20 pg/mL with suppressed LH.
Trough LH < 0.3; stimulated LH < 2 (ideal), > 4-5 = inadequate.
Estradiol not < 5 (over-suppression) nor > 30 (pubertal).

items.title: Critical teaching pearls
items.content: Trough labs are essential — drug is lowest just before the next dose, so a suppressed trough means suppression across the whole interval. LH rises before estradiol, so monitor LH for early escape. BMI matters — obese children may need higher doses. Never accept persistent vaginal bleeding on treatment; it means the ovary is still making estrogen (inadequate dose or a peripheral source). GH excess can masquerade as failure (suppressed estradiol but advancing bone age → check IGF-1, consider McCune-Albright). Monthly dosing is safer for initial/rescue suppression; switch to 3-monthly only after confirmed sustained suppression. Zero estradiol is not the goal. If suppression fails despite maximal agonist, reconsider the diagnosis (peripheral precocious puberty). Document suppression at least once with a GnRH stimulation test. And triptorelin may work when leuprolide fails — different agonists have different binding kinetics.
keyPoints: Check trough labs; LH escapes before estradiol; never accept ongoing bleeding.
Suppressed estradiol but advancing bone age → check IGF-1 (GH excess/MAS).
If maximal agonist fails, reconsider peripheral PP; triptorelin may succeed where leuprolide fails.

items.question: Why does a GnRH agonist cause an initial flare before it suppresses the axis?
items.answer: Because the agonist initially does exactly what GnRH does — it occupies and activates the GnRH receptor — but continuously rather than in pulses. For the first 1-2 weeks the gonadotrophs respond to this stimulation with a surge of LH and FSH, driving a rise in estradiol (the flare: vaginal bleeding, breast tenderness, mood changes). Only with sustained, unbroken occupancy do the receptors become internalised and depleted below the threshold needed for signalling, at which point the gonadotroph becomes refractory and gonadotropins and estradiol fall to prepubertal levels. The flare is therefore the unavoidable agonist phase that precedes downregulation, and in a child with a high baseline (or a sensitive uterus) it may need covering with a progestin, an aromatase inhibitor, or antagonist pre-treatment.
keyPoints: Continuous agonist first stimulates the receptor (flare, weeks 1-2).
Sustained occupancy then internalises/depletes receptors → suppression.
Cover the flare when needed (progestin, aromatase inhibitor, antagonist pre-treatment).

items.question: How should you manage a girl with unsuppressed LH on leuprolide 11.25 mg every 90 days?
items.answer: Act at the next injection, not after another 3-month cycle. First confirm the escape (trough LH, and ideally a GnRH-stimulated LH) and check estradiol; if estradiol is pubertal despite the dose, look hard for a peripheral source. Then switch to monthly dosing — leuprolide 7.5 mg (or 11.25 mg) every 28 days, or triptorelin 3.75 mg monthly if leuprolide may be partially resistant — and recheck LH and estradiol at 4 weeks at the trough. If still unsuppressed, escalate stepwise (11.25 → 15 mg monthly; or 22.5 mg every 2 months; or a histrelin implant), consider adding an aromatase inhibitor to block peripheral estradiol while central control is optimised, and verify compliance and injection technique. Once suppressed, hold monthly dosing for 6-12 months and confirm with a stimulated LH before considering a return to 3-monthly. Throughout, watch for over-suppression (growth velocity, estradiol not below ~5 pg/mL).
keyPoints: Confirm escape (trough + stimulated LH); exclude a peripheral source.
Switch to monthly now; recheck at 4-week trough; escalate stepwise if needed.
Hold monthly 6-12 months, confirm with stimulated LH; watch for over-suppression.

items.question: List flare-prevention options beyond medroxyprogesterone, and the goal of each.
items.answer: 1) GnRH-antagonist pre-treatment (cetrorelix/ganirelix/degarelix) for 1-3 days before and 1-2 weeks into agonist therapy — the most definitive option, eliminating the flare by immediate receptor blockade (limited by cost/availability). 2) Aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole/letrozole) started with the agonist — does not stop the LH surge but blocks estradiol synthesis, preventing the estrogen-mediated bleed. 3) Tamoxifen (SERM) — blocks estrogen action at target tissues (breast tenderness/mood, not bleeding). 4) 'Step-up' daily subcutaneous leuprolide (20 mcg/kg/day) for 2-4 weeks then depot — faster, more uniform downregulation and a shorter flare. 5) Start with a monthly (not 3-monthly) depot — higher peak levels for quicker downregulation. 6) Cyproterone acetate (where available) — dual progestin/anti-androgen cover. 7) NSAIDs — symptomatic relief of cramping/bleeding only. Medroxyprogesterone itself (5-10 mg/day PO or 150 mg IM, from ~1 week before through 2-4 weeks after) remains the traditional agent.
keyPoints: GnRH antagonist pre-treatment = most definitive (no flare).
Aromatase inhibitor / tamoxifen neutralise the estrogen effect; daily step-up or monthly start shortens the flare.
Cyproterone (dual action) and NSAIDs (symptomatic) are adjuncts.

items.stem: When does the flare from a GnRH agonist typically occur, and why?
options: Months later, due to drug accumulation
In the first 1-2 weeks, because continuous receptor stimulation initially surges LH/FSH before downregulation
Only if the dose is too low
It does not occur with depot formulations
items.explanation: Continuous (non-pulsatile) agonist stimulation first activates the gonadotroph, surging LH/FSH and estradiol over weeks 1-2 (the flare), before sustained occupancy downregulates the receptors and suppresses the axis.

items.stem: Which approach most completely prevents the GnRH-agonist flare?
options: Starting a low dose and titrating up slowly
GnRH-antagonist pre-treatment, which blocks the receptor immediately so no flare occurs
Giving the agonist only once
Adding a vitamin supplement
items.explanation: A GnRH antagonist competitively blocks the receptor for immediate suppression with no flare; the agonist is then introduced to establish long-term downregulation. Cost and pediatric availability limit its routine use.

items.stem: What is an appropriate medroxyprogesterone regimen for flare prophylaxis?
options: A single dose on the day of the agonist only
5-10 mg/day orally (or 150 mg IM depot), started ~1 week before the agonist and continued 2-4 weeks
100 mg/day for 6 months
It is given after the flare has resolved
items.explanation: MPA 5-10 mg/day PO (or 150 mg IM) starting about a week before the first agonist dose and continuing 2-4 weeks covers the whole flare window by suppressing the gonadotroph and ovary.

items.stem: A girl on leuprolide 11.25 mg every 90 days has unsuppressed LH. What is the best immediate step?
options: Wait the full 3 months, then reassess
Switch to monthly dosing at the next injection and recheck a trough LH/estradiol at 4 weeks
Stop all therapy
Double the interval to every 6 months
items.explanation: Escape requires prompt intensification: switch to monthly dosing now (don't wait another cycle) and confirm suppression at the 4-week trough, escalating the dose if still unsuppressed.

items.stem: What is a harm of over-suppressing estradiol toward zero in a child on GnRH-agonist therapy?
options: Improved final height
Growth deceleration and reduced bone mineral density, because some estradiol is needed for the growth plate and bone
Faster bone-age advancement
No effect at all
items.explanation: Prepubertal children need some estradiol (with GH/IGF-1) for normal growth and bone mineralisation; driving it below ~5 pg/mL can slow growth and reduce bone density. The target is the prepubertal range, not zero.

items.stem: Which marker rises first when GnRH-agonist suppression begins to escape?
options: Estradiol rises first
LH rises before estradiol, making it the earliest warning of escape
They rise simultaneously and identically
Neither changes until clinical progression
items.explanation: LH (central output) rises before estradiol (the downstream ovarian response), so monitoring LH gives an early warning to intervene before clinical breakthrough occurs.

items.stem: On therapy, LH is suppressed but estradiol is pubertal and the child is progressing. What does this indicate?
options: The GnRH-agonist dose is too low
A peripheral (gonadotropin-independent) estrogen source — e.g. McCune-Albright, a functional ovarian cyst, adrenal, or exogenous estrogen
The lab is always wrong
Normal suppression
items.explanation: If LH is genuinely suppressed yet estradiol stays pubertal, the estrogen is not gonadotropin-driven — look for a peripheral source. Escalating the GnRH agonist will not help.

items.stem: What is the gold-standard test to confirm adequate GnRH-agonist suppression?
options: A single random estradiol
A GnRH-stimulated LH (a peak < ~2-4 IU/L confirms suppression; > 4-5 is inadequate)
Bone age alone
Height percentile
items.explanation: A GnRH-stimulated LH is the definitive measure: an ideal peak < 2 IU/L (acceptable < 4) confirms suppression, whereas > 4-5 IU/L indicates inadequate suppression requiring a management change.

items.stem: What is the target estradiol on GnRH-agonist therapy for central precocious puberty?
options: As close to zero as possible
Prepubertal (~10-20 pg/mL) — low but not abolished
Pubertal (>30 pg/mL)
Estradiol is irrelevant
items.explanation: The sweet spot is prepubertal estradiol (~10-20 pg/mL) with suppressed LH — enough for normal prepubertal growth and bone mineralisation without pubertal progression; persistently < 5 pg/mL risks harm.

items.stem: If suppression fails despite escalating to maximal GnRH-agonist dosing, what should you reconsider?
options: That the child simply needs more time
Whether this is actually peripheral precocious puberty (e.g. McCune-Albright or a functional cyst), where the ovary does not need LH
That estradiol is unimportant
Stopping all monitoring
items.explanation: A GnRH agonist cannot control autonomous peripheral estrogen production; persistent failure despite maximal dosing should prompt re-evaluation for a peripheral (gonadotropin-independent) source.

items.stem: Why should suppression be checked at the trough (just before the next dose) rather than soon after injection?
options: Because labs are cheaper then
Because drug levels are lowest just before the next dose, so a suppressed trough means suppression across the whole interval
Because estradiol is highest at the trough
Trough timing does not matter
items.explanation: The trough is the most vulnerable point of the dosing interval; if LH/estradiol are suppressed at trough, they are suppressed throughout, whereas a post-injection sample could falsely reassure.

items.statement: The GnRH-agonist flare occurs in the first 1-2 weeks, before receptor downregulation and suppression.
items.explanation: True. Continuous agonist stimulation first surges LH/FSH/estradiol (the flare) over 1-2 weeks before sustained occupancy downregulates the receptors and suppresses the axis.

items.statement: The goal of GnRH-agonist therapy is to drive estradiol to zero.
items.explanation: False. Some estradiol is needed for normal prepubertal growth and bone mineralisation; the target is the prepubertal range (~10-20 pg/mL), and persistently < 5 pg/mL risks harm.

items.statement: LH typically rises before estradiol during treatment escape, making it the earliest warning.
items.explanation: True. LH (central output) rises before the downstream estradiol response, so monitoring LH provides early detection of escape.

items.statement: Suppressed LH with a pubertal estradiol suggests a peripheral (gonadotropin-independent) estrogen source.
items.explanation: True. If LH is suppressed yet estradiol remains pubertal, the estrogen is autonomous/peripheral (e.g. McCune-Albright, ovarian cyst, adrenal, exogenous), not a dosing failure.

items.statement: For unsuppressed LH on a 3-monthly depot, it is best to wait the full 3 months before changing anything.
items.explanation: False. Escape should be intensified promptly — switch to monthly dosing at the next injection, because every month of inadequate suppression advances bone age irreversibly.

items.statement: A GnRH-stimulated LH below about 2-4 IU/L is definitive evidence of adequate suppression.
items.explanation: True. The stimulated LH is the gold standard; a peak < 2 (ideal) to < 4 IU/L confirms suppression, while > 4-5 indicates inadequate suppression.

items.statement: Persistent vaginal bleeding while on GnRH-agonist therapy is acceptable and needs no action.
items.explanation: False. Ongoing bleeding means the ovary is still producing estrogen — the dose is inadequate or there is a peripheral source — and must be investigated and corrected.

items.assertion: A girl with unsuppressed LH on 3-monthly leuprolide should be switched to monthly dosing without waiting another cycle.
items.reason: Every month of inadequate suppression allows irreversible bone-age advancement and loss of adult-height potential.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because the harm of continued escape (irreversible skeletal maturation) accrues monthly, the dosing should be intensified immediately rather than after another 3-month interval.
